patient.name: Walter Kowalski
patient.chiefComplaint: Acute shortness of breath, woke up unable to breathe

history.hpi: 72-year-old male with known CHF (EF 25%), awoke at about 02:30 with sudden severe shortness of breath. Unable to lie flat. Wife states he has been non-compliant with his fluid restriction for the past week. No chest pain, no fever, no recent medication changes. Blood pressure 'has been high' per wife's home cuff.
history.lastOralIntake: Dinner at 18:00 yesterday — large portion of leftover stew
history.events: Woke up acutely short of breath after being asleep for about 3 hours. Sat up immediately. Wife called 911 when he became too breathless to speak.

scene.dispatch: Medic 14, respond code 3 to 3214 Garfield Avenue, for a 72-year-old male with difficulty breathing, known heart failure.
scene.location: Single-family home, main floor bedroom. Patient sitting bolt upright on the edge of the bed in pajamas.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Narrow bedroom, will need to extricate via the main hallway.
scene.bystanders: Wife is present with his medication list and will accompany to the ED
visualCues: Elderly male sitting bolt upright on the edge of the bed, gripping the mattress
Pink-tinged frothy sputum at the lips
Accessory muscle use with every breath
JVD visible from across the room
Scale in the bathroom showing 6-lb weight gain over the past week

phases.name: Arrival — Hypertensive Flash Pulmonary Edema
phases.description: Classic hypertensive flash pulmonary edema. This is a problem of volume DISTRIBUTION (afterload) more than total body fluid overload. Treatment centerpiece: CPAP for positive pressure ventilation to redistribute fluid, and aggressive nitroglycerin for afterload reduction. Do NOT rush to intubate — medical therapy works quickly.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent; audible gurgling with each breath; pink frothy sputum at lips
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Rapid, labored at 32/min; diffuse crackles to the apices bilaterally; accessory muscle use

skin.color: pale with dusky lips, peripheral cyanosis

otherFindings: JVD to the angle of the jaw
History of medication non-compliance and dietary indiscretion

expectedActions.action: Keep patient in upright position; do NOT lay flat
Apply CPAP starting at 5 cmH2O, titrate up to 10 cmH2O as tolerated; this is the single most important intervention
expectedActions.rationale: Positive pressure recruits alveoli, improves V/Q matching, and reduces preload and afterload — it directly treats the pathophysiology.

expectedActions.action: Actively coach the patient through CPAP application — severe SOB patients often panic initially; never break the seal
Administer Nitroglycerin 0.8 mg SL (2 tablets or 2 sprays) given SBP ≥160 mmHg; repeat every 5 minutes for SBP >100
expectedActions.rationale: High-dose nitroglycerin is the cornerstone of hypertensive CHF. At high doses it reduces both preload AND afterload, improving stroke volume.

expectedActions.action: Confirm no phosphodiesterase inhibitor (sildenafil, tadalafil, vardenafil) use within 48 hours before nitro administration
Apply cardiac monitor, SpO2, waveform capnography; obtain 12-lead EKG to rule out STEMI as precipitant
Do NOT proceed to intubation — medical therapy works rapidly in flash pulmonary edema
Do NOT administer furosemide — it provides no immediate benefit and the problem is distribution, not total body water

phases.name: Responding to CPAP and Nitroglycerin
phases.description: Within 10-15 minutes of CPAP and repeated nitroglycerin, the patient's condition improves markedly. SpO2 climbs, respiratory rate decreases, speaks in longer phrases, and BP begins to normalize. Classic rapid response to afterload reduction.
phases.triggerCondition: Improvement after CPAP and 2-3 doses of nitroglycerin

clinicalPresentation.breathing: CPAP in place, respiratory rate decreasing, accessory muscle use reduced
clinicalPresentation.circulation: BP trending down from afterload reduction
otherFindings: Crackles now only bibasilar (improved from apical)
12-lead EKG shows sinus tach, LVH by voltage, no STEMI
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'I can breathe a little better now'

expectedActions.action: Continue Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL every 5 minutes for SBP >100; consider IV nitroglycerin infusion titrated to effect if available
Do NOT discontinue CPAP during transport or transfer — maintain seal until ED bedside
Reassess BP after each nitroglycerin dose; hold for SBP <100
Transport to appropriate ED, preferably with cardiac catheterization capability
Continuous reassessment — be prepared to escalate airway if patient deteriorates, but watch for continued improvement

phases.name: Branch: Intubation Instead of CPAP
phases.description: Crew perceives the severity and jumps to intubation before trying CPAP and nitroglycerin. Patient is now on positive pressure ventilation via ETT (same benefit as CPAP) but has been exposed to the risks of RSI and loss of an easily-reversible trajectory. This branch teaches restraint in flash pulmonary edema.
phases.triggerCondition: Premature RSI without trial of CPAP and nitro

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Hypertensive but with post-induction drop concern
otherFindings: Patient could likely have avoided intubation with CPAP and nitro
Now committed to mechanical ventilation and ICU

expectedActions.action: Recognize that CPAP should have been first-line; document reasoning for future QI
Continue nitroglycerin therapy even post-intubation for afterload reduction

criticalActions: Recognize hypertensive flash pulmonary edema (BP, JVD, crackles, pink frothy sputum)
Apply CPAP with good coaching, starting 5 cmH2O and titrating up
Administer Nitroglycerin 0.8 mg SL given SBP >160, repeat q5min for SBP >100
Confirm no PDE-5 inhibitor use prior to nitroglycerin
Obtain 12-lead EKG to rule out STEMI precipitant
Do NOT discontinue CPAP in transit
Do NOT intubate as first-line — medical therapy works quickly
expectedActions: Serial BP monitoring after each nitro dose
Reassess lung sounds and work of breathing
Consider IV nitroglycerin infusion if available
bonusActions: Articulate that high-dose nitro reduces afterload as well as preload
Articulate that pulmonary edema is a distribution problem, not a total-body-water problem
Coach the patient through CPAP mask application actively

learningObjectives: Recognize and treat hypertensive flash pulmonary edema
Apply CPAP as first-line therapy, coaching for tolerance
Use high-dose nitroglycerin for afterload reduction in CHF
Understand why furosemide is not prehospital-indicated
Resist premature intubation when medical therapy is working
discussionQuestions: Why is CPAP so effective in pulmonary edema? What is the physiology?
Why do we use HIGH dose nitroglycerin in CHF, not the routine chest-pain dose?
Why is furosemide NOT first-line in the prehospital setting?
When would intubation actually be indicated in flash pulmonary edema?
What are the signs that the patient's hypertensive emergency is resolving?
commonPitfalls: Intubating too early before CPAP has had a chance
Under-dosing nitroglycerin (0.4 mg instead of 0.8 mg in hypertensive patients)
Giving furosemide because it 'is the heart failure drug'
Breaking the CPAP seal for assessments or transfers
Missing a STEMI precipitant on the 12-lead
keyTakeaways: CPAP + nitro are the afterload-reducers — use them aggressively
Flash pulmonary edema is hypertensive physiology; normalizing BP normalizes the lungs
Diuretics are a hospital intervention, not a prehospital one
Coach, don't force — mask tolerance is what makes CPAP work
